select_when: A team is persisting mainly because of prior investment, history, or identity rather than because the future economics still justify recommitment.
The real decision is whether we would start again today from current facts, not whether we can emotionally justify past effort.
A project, relationship, or investment needs to be reevaluated from prospective value instead of from already-incurred cost.
The next useful move is to separate what is irrecoverable from what still has live future upside.
avoid_when: Anti-sunk-cost logic is being used as cover for reckless abandonment without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or remaining upside.
The team already understands the sunk cost clearly and now mainly needs execution on the chosen exit or recommitment path.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model centered on future-oriented recommitment testing and anti-escalation discipline rather than on generic loss language.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor behavioral-econ sprawl because the raw source is stronger on restart-from-today framing and anti-legacy-justification discipline.
open_questions: The output type compresses a source that also spans finance, organizational drift, and switching-cost considerations.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit on reevaluating from current facts, asking whether we would start again today, and resisting prior-investment inertia.

avoid_when.note: Both anti-activation conditions are grounded in source warnings that anti-sunk-cost logic can also be misused to rationalize careless exit.

input_type.note: The source clearly activates on escalation driven by legacy investment, while the typed input remains a compact reviewed label.

output_type.note: The source yields a reset decision based on future value; the output label compresses that into an operational artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Sunk-cost-fallacy is strongest as counterfactual and diagnostic reevaluation, while the taxonomy labels remain normalized.
